author: @ClownWhisper
text:
this is when a solder sucking station comes in handy.  they are very expensive though....

I have used heat guns to get the job done as well

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text: Clown Whisper cool, I couldn't personally get enough heat with a heatgun on these boards

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: if there is that much copper in those boards, a bit of clean up and sulfuric acid could be used to start the reclaiming process by making copper sulfate could be made for turning it into nano materials on the cheap :)

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text: Thomas Anderson good idea but there isn't really that much copper in absolute terms, but in relative terms the boards have very heavy copper layers and that makes a big difference in desoldering

author: @JustinHallPlus
text: Good recycling!  Don't breathe the dust though.

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text: Justin Hall yeah I'll probably wear a mask next time ;)

author: @grumpysdiy
text: I've found a small butane torch makes quick work of desoldering

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text: joe Camel cheers

author: @DigGil3
text: Great! I gotta try this out. Playstation2 controller thumbsticks don't seem so impossible to remove now!

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube

author: @yvairguy
text: Heat a tray of silica sand, lay the board on the sand and the parts pull/fall right off. At least that's how we did it..... wow 30 years ago, now I feel old :/

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text: Jeff Jones thanks, very interesting,  not heard this method before, we do have a kiln in the lab so I wonder ...

author: @danajohnson3799
text: We use a large solder pot.

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text: Dana Johnson cool

author: @jasonsteedley9566
text: Next time save time and effort, purchase chipquick alloy used in smd reworking and apply a touch to each point. Stays liquid for a long time then just lift the board off. Best component removal aid and not expensive.

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text: jason steedley chipquik is cool stuff, I wonder how easy it would be to homebrew :)

replies.author: @jasonsteedley9566
replies.text: It's fairly inexpensive I'm one to save time and effort and just buy in bulk. I don't think in home I could make a 180 degree melting point alloy. I use it Alot to do hot air desoldering and soldering esp with smart phone charging ports, multi point micro soldering with out damaging any pads or bridges.

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text: Nice, I'd like to see how it performs on heavy pcbs.

replies.author: @jasonsteedley9566
replies.text: I have a stock pile of alloy for my phone repair store I'll have to pull out an old pcb and make a video for you. Used it yesterday for a 80's jeep wrangler ECM board replacing old old capacitors. Works like a charm bc no space to get solder iron under or around capacitors but hot air at low temp is enough to do this with out damaging plastic connectors.

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text: Sounds fantastic mate, desoldering the impossible to reach :) looking forward to seeing the vid

author: @bridgendesar
text:
What if you used a hole saw, you can get small ones that are diamond tipped.
Just go down enough to cut through the copper (removing thermal sink), You'd twist off the leg of the cap if you went right through though.

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text: bridgendesar interesting idea, i gues if it was really high rpm it could cut through without twisting?

author: @crafter2u
text: i just use a torch and a blow gun

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text: Dennis Peacock that'll do it :)

author: @pypes84
text: Poor lad, there was a quick release on that vice.

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text: pypes84 haha, steve pointed that out to me just after I'd done it. Now I know :)

author: @BioDynamicIndustries
text:
There may perhaps be a better way of doing this. If you're going to desolder frequently then either make a reflow oven out of a toaster oven (to liquefy the solder just so you can turn the board over and knock them out)..  or.. copycat what's done professionally and make a pool of melted solder maybe a quarter inch high and just lay the bottom end of the board in and yank the pieces out, So think of a hot plate with walls which melts and holds an ingot of solder. Screw the solder sucker.

Btw, the new guy explains things really really well. I like his videos!

replies.author: @nicolaarnold5921
replies.text: Sean Lutz incidentally I do have a reflow oven ;) The melted solder bed sounds pretty radical, I'd like to see that in action, perhaps a bit overkill for this heath robinson reclaimation operation :)

replies.author: @BioDynamicIndustries
replies.text:
Nicola Arnold 
works every quick, just set it above the surface of the solder and pull out the through-hole components. Surface mount ic's require you to submerge it further but overall it really doesn't overheat the components since you scale the temp right at the melting point rather than hit it dead headed with an iron. Tbh, I haven't had any major fume issue either.

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text: Sean Lutz it does sound rather awesome, do you reuse the solder in the bed each time? do you have to add extra flux?

author: @AustralViking
text: One thing I don't understand is, how does the heat get conducted away so quickly into the copper plate, after all it is not touching the pins of the capacitor, ((well maybe one) else it would short it out.?

replies.author: @nicolaarnold5921
replies.text: Andrew B good point, only two of the pins are directly connected to the groundplane, they were probably the second two I did in the video come to think of it. The non-groundplane pins tend to be dificult too however as the pcb itself will conduct heat through to the groundplane too, but good point :)
